summary: Updated the root Makefile to make several compiler behaviors configurable from the command line. The PR added `REQUIRE_PROTOS` and `MSG_STYLE` variables, changed `-msgstyle gcc` to use `$(MSG_STYLE)`, conditionally adds `-requireprotos`, and adds `-nofail` when `MAX_ERRORS` is `0`. This improves build/debug ergonomics without touching decompiled source.

key_files.reason: Only changed file; adds command-line-overridable variables and derives compiler flags from them.

what_changed: Added `REQUIRE_PROTOS ?= 0` as a Makefile variable, allowing developers to opt into prototype enforcement from the command line.
Added `MSG_STYLE ?= gcc` as a Makefile variable, replacing the hard-coded `-msgstyle gcc` compiler flag with `-msgstyle $(MSG_STYLE)`.
Added conditional `CFLAGS += -requireprotos` when `REQUIRE_PROTOS` is set to `1`.
Added conditional `CFLAGS += -nofail` when `MAX_ERRORS` is `0`, matching the Makefile comment that `0` means no maximum.
Kept existing matching-related behavior around `NON_MATCHING` and `-DMUST_MATCH` intact.
smart_moves: Used Make's `?=` assignment style for new knobs, preserving defaults while allowing command-line overrides such as `make MSG_STYLE=...` or `make REQUIRE_PROTOS=1`.
Converted a hard-coded compiler presentation option into a variable without changing the default behavior.
Made `MAX_ERRORS=0` explicitly translate into `-nofail`, which aligns the compiler invocation with the documented no-maximum semantics.
Kept the change localized to the Makefile, minimizing risk to object matching and source organization.
decomp_lessons: For decompilation repositories, build flags that affect diagnostics or enforcement should be command-line configurable so contributors can tighten checks locally without forcing the whole project to adopt them immediately.
Use Makefile defaults with `?=` for optional workflows; this keeps CI/default builds stable while giving local developers more control.
Avoid hard-coding compiler diagnostic formatting when tools, editors, or scripts may need alternate message styles.
When a numeric Makefile setting has a sentinel value like `0 = no maximum`, make sure the generated compiler flags explicitly implement that sentinel behavior.
naming_conventions: `REQUIRE_PROTOS` follows the existing uppercase Makefile variable style used by `GENERATE_MAP`, `NON_MATCHING`, `EPILOGUE_PROCESS`, and `SKIP_CHECK`.
`MSG_STYLE` names the configurable value rather than a specific compiler flag, allowing it to map cleanly to `-msgstyle $(MSG_STYLE)`.
`MAX_ERRORS` retains the existing documented convention where `0` means no maximum.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: No direct assembly matching tactic was introduced.
The Makefile still gates `-DMUST_MATCH` on `NON_MATCHING != 1`, preserving the existing matching/non-matching build split.
The added flags are diagnostic/enforcement related and should not be assumed to affect generated code based on the provided diff.
review_feedback: No issue comments or review comments were present in the provided PR slice.
One review is recorded, but no review body or specific feedback was included in the context.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that enable or discuss `REQUIRE_PROTOS`.
Search for uses of `MSG_STYLE` in CI or developer scripts.
Look for subsequent Makefile changes involving `MAX_ERRORS` and `-nofail`.
Compare this with other PRs that convert hard-coded compiler flags into command-line-overridable Make variables.
